Week 42 · Special Senses Histology of the Globe & Retina LO 42D
Overview Eyelid Fibrous Layer Uvea Lens Retina Rods & Cones Optic Nerve Gallery
LO 42D

The Eyeball — Orientation & Organization

Demonstrate, using histological images, the layers, cells, and structures of the eyeball and orbit-associated structures, and be able to relate the cells to their function.

The bony orbit houses a fibrous globe containing a photosensitive retina. Light passing through the anterior eye strikes the retina, which converts it into neural code transmitted via the thalamus to the visual cortex.

Three Chambers

🔵 Anterior Chamber Between cornea & iris Contains aqueous humor; bounded anteriorly by cornea, posteriorly by iris
🟣 Posterior Chamber Between iris & lens Narrow rim; lens effectively fills most of it. Aqueous produced here by ciliary processes
⚪ Vitreous Chamber Behind lens Largest; contains jelly-like vitreous fluid that maintains globe shape and keeps retina applied

Three Concentric Layers (Tunics)

LayerStructuresCharacterFunction
Corneoscleral (Fibrous)
Cornea (ant. 1/5) + Sclera (post. 4/5)
Tap to reveal
Dense collagen; cornea transparent, sclera opaque
Tap to reveal
Structural support & refractive surface
Tap to reveal
Uvea (Vascular)
Choroid + Ciliary body + Iris
Tap to reveal
Highly vascular; rich in melanocytes
Tap to reveal
Nourish retina; produce aqueous; control light entry
Tap to reveal
Retina (Neural)
10 distinct layers of neurons, glia, & pigment
Tap to reveal
Embryologically brain tissue; unmyelinated axons
Tap to reveal
Phototransduction & initial visual processing
Tap to reveal
Fig 1 (Manual) Midsagittal H&E section of a primate eye — all chambers, layers, and structures labeled Medical Histology Manual, Week 42
Fig 3 (Manual) Three tunics of the globe: retina, choroid, sclera. Tenon's capsule seen externally (H&E) Medical Histology Manual, Week 42

The Eyelid (Palpebra)

The eyelid protects the globe and participates in tear spreading. Its structure transitions from keratinized stratified squamous anteriorly to non-keratinized epithelium on the posterior conjunctival surface.

Layers of the Eyelid

Skin (keratinized SSE) → Subcutaneous layer → Orbicularis oculi (skeletal m.) → Tarsal plate + Tarsal glands → Palpebral conjunctiva

Glands of the Eyelid

GlandTypeLocationSecretion / Function
Meibomian (Tarsal)
Modified sebaceous (holocrine)
Tap
Embedded in fibrous tarsal plate
Tap
Sebaceous oil — prevents tear evaporation; occlusion → chalazion
Tap
Zeis
Sebaceous (associated with cilia)
Tap
At eyelash follicles (lid margin)
Tap
Sebaceous secretion lubricating lash follicle; occlusion → external hordeolum (stye)
Tap
Moll
Apocrine sweat gland
Tap
At eyelash follicles (lid margin)
Tap
Watery secretion contributing to tear film
Tap

Conjunctiva

The conjunctiva lines the posterior surface of the eyelid (palpebral conjunctiva) and reflects onto the globe (bulbar conjunctiva). The transition zone is the fornix. Epithelium is non-keratinized stratified squamous with scattered goblet cells that secrete mucin for the tear film.

Muscles of the Eyelid

Orbicularis Oculi

  • Circular skeletal muscle forming a sheet across entire lid
  • Closes eyelid (blink, forced closure)
  • Innervated by CN VII (facial nerve)
  • Seen in cross-section as grouped striated muscle profiles

Levator Palpebrae Superioris & Müller's (Tarsal) Muscle

  • Levator palpebrae superioris: striated, elevates lid; innervated by CN III
  • Müller's muscle: smooth muscle slips attached to superior tarsus; sympathetically innervated → ptosis in Horner's syndrome
⚠️ Clinical — Horner's Syndrome
Müller's muscle (superior tarsal muscle) is sympathetically innervated. Loss of sympathetic tone → partial ptosis (2–3 mm drooping) + miosis (dilator pupillae also affected). Full ptosis indicates CN III palsy, not Horner's.
Fig 2A (Manual) Upper lid: orbicularis oculi, tarsal plate, tarsal (Meibomian) glands (H&E) Medical Histology Manual, Week 42
Fig 2B (Manual) Eyelash end of upper lid — palpebral conjunctiva, sebaceous (Zeis) glands (H&E) Medical Histology Manual, Week 42
Fig 2C (Manual) Base of eyelid — tarsal glands surrounded by fibrous tarsal plate (H&E) Medical Histology Manual, Week 42

Outer (Fibrous / Corneoscleral) Tunic

Dense collagenous layer forming the structural shell of the globe. The posterior 4/5 is opaque sclera; the anterior 1/5 is transparent cornea. Both share the same basic composition — parallel laminae of collagen fibers — but transparency is conferred to the cornea by extreme regularity of fibril spacing.

Sclera

Sub-layerComposition / FeatureClinical / Functional Note
Tenon's Capsule
Loose connective tissue fascia (episclera); surrounds sclera and extra-ocular muscles
Tap
Space of Tenon allows globe rotation; inflammation → tightening impedes movement
Tap
Sclera Proper
Dense irregular collagen; flattened fibroblasts; avascular (receives nutrition by diffusion)
Tap
Tendons of extraocular muscles attach here; appears blue in osteogenesis imperfecta (thinning)
Tap
Suprachoroid Lamina (Lamina Fusca)
Thin layer of loose CT between sclera proper and choroid; contains melanocytes
Tap
Potential space for suprachoroidal hemorrhage or drug delivery
Tap

Cornea — Five Layers

The cornea provides the greatest refractive power of the eye (~43D, vs ~17D for lens). Its transparency requires avascularity, regular collagen fibril arrangement, and an active endothelial pump to maintain dehydration.

① Epithelium → ② Bowman's Membrane → ③ Substantia Propria (Stroma) → ④ Descemet's Membrane → ⑤ Endothelium
LayerHistologyFunction / Key Fact
① Epithelium
Non-keratinized stratified squamous (5–6 cell layers); sits on BM
Tap
Barrier; highly regenerative (heals without scarring). Damaged in corneal abrasion
Tap
② Bowman's Membrane
Acellular condensed collagen; permanent, does not regenerate
Tap
Scarring when damaged; breached in recurrent erosion syndrome
Tap
③ Substantia Propria
90% of corneal thickness; 200–250 parallel collagen lamellae with regular 64 nm spacing; keratocytes (modified fibroblasts) between lamellae
Tap
Regular fibril spacing → destructive interference of scattered light → transparency; disruption (edema, scar) → opacity
Tap
④ Descemet's Membrane
Thick BM (Type IV collagen + laminin) secreted by endothelium; thickens with age
Tap
Rupture → hydrops (acute corneal edema) in keratoconus; Haab's striae = breaks in congenital glaucoma
Tap
⑤ Endothelium
Simple cuboidal; Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase pumps keep stroma dehydrated
Tap
Non-regenerative in adults (cannot divide); loss → corneal edema (Fuchs' endothelial dystrophy)
Tap
🔬 Clinical — Red Eye & Corneal Abrasion
In a corneal abrasion, the epithelium is damaged (heals rapidly, no scar). In "red eye," the inflamed layer is typically the conjunctiva (conjunctivitis). The cornea is densely innervated by CN V₁ branches → exquisitely painful when damaged.
Fig 4 (Manual) Section through corneoscleral junction — conjunctiva, sclera, ciliary body, lens edge (H&E) Medical Histology Manual, Week 42
Fig 5 (Manual) Five layers of the cornea: epithelium → Bowman's → substantia propria → Descemet's → endothelium (H&E) Medical Histology Manual, Week 42

Middle (Uveal / Vascular) Tunic

The uvea is the pigmented, vascular middle layer. It has three distinct regions: the choroid (posterior), ciliary body (equatorial), and iris (anterior). Together they provide nutrition, produce aqueous humor, and regulate pupil size.

Choroid

A dense vascular layer between retina and sclera that supplies the outer 2/3 of the retina (photoreceptors + RPE) by diffusion. The inner retina (ganglion cells, bipolar cells) is nourished by the central retinal artery.

Sub-layerHistologyFunction
Glassy Membrane (Bruch's)
Thin, hyaline BM; innermost; sits directly beneath RPE
Tap
Separates RPE from choriocapillaris; drusen accumulation here → macular degeneration
Tap
Choriocapillary Layer
Dense network of fenestrated capillaries; innermost vascular layer
Tap
Nourishes outer retina / RPE; choroidal neovascularization → wet AMD
Tap
Blood Vessel Layer
Medium and large arteries/veins (posterior ciliary artery branches); surrounded by matrix rich in melanocytes
Tap
High blood flow (highest per unit weight in body); thermoregulates retina
Tap
Suprachoroid Layer
Loose CT with melanocytes; merges with sclera
Tap
Transitional zone; potential surgical access route
Tap
Fig 6 (Manual) Choroid layers: Bruch's membrane, choriocapillary, blood vessel, suprachoroid, and sclera (H&E) Medical Histology Manual, Week 42

Ciliary Body

The ciliary body is a thickened ring of tissue that marks the transition between choroid and iris. It performs two critical functions: accommodation (via ciliary muscle → zonular fibers → lens shape) and aqueous humor production (via ciliary processes).

ComponentHistologyFunction
Ciliary Muscle
Smooth muscle (3 orientations: longitudinal, radial, circular); dominant component
Tap
Contraction → relaxes zonule fibers → lens rounds up → near focus (accommodation). Parasympathetic via CN III
Tap
Ciliary Processes
Finger-like vascular projections into posterior chamber; lined by double-layer pigmented epithelium
Tap
Secrete aqueous humor by active Na⁺ transport + ultrafiltration. Target of glaucoma drops (carbonic anhydrase inhibitors, β-blockers, prostaglandins)
Tap
Zonular Fibers (of Zinn)
Fibrillin microfibrils extending from ciliary processes to lens equator
Tap
Suspend lens; hold it flat at rest; relax during accommodation. Defect in Marfan's → lens subluxation
Tap
Iridocorneal Angle & Canal of Schlemm
Trabecular meshwork + scleral venous sinus (canal of Schlemm); at angle between iris root and cornea
Tap
Drainage route for aqueous humor. Blockage → elevated IOP → open-angle (trabecular) or closed-angle glaucoma
Tap
💧 Aqueous Humor Circulation
Produced at ciliary processes → posterior chamber → through pupil → anterior chamber → iridocorneal angle → trabecular meshwork → canal of Schlemm → episcleral veins. Normal IOP = 10–21 mmHg.
Fig 7 (Manual) Ciliary body, iridocorneal angle, scleral venous sinus (Canal of Schlemm), ciliary processes, zonule fibers (H&E) Medical Histology Manual, Week 42
Fig 8 (Manual) Magnified ciliary processes — pigmented epithelium, smooth muscle, melanocytes in ciliary body (H&E) Medical Histology Manual, Week 42

Iris

A thin, contractile diaphragm that regulates pupil size and thus the amount of light entering the eye.

Layer / ComponentHistologyFunction
Endothelium (Anterior Border)
Modified flattened fibroblasts; incomplete, no true endothelium — directly exposed to aqueous
Tap
Forms anterior border of iris; fibroblasts merge into trabecular meshwork at iridocorneal angle
Tap
Stroma
Loose CT; chromatophores (pigment-laden fibroblasts), collagen, blood vessels
Tap
Pigment content determines iris color — brown iris has dense chromatophores, blue iris sparse
Tap
Sphincter Pupillae
Circular smooth muscle ring at pupillary margin (derived from ectoderm)
Tap
Constricts pupil (miosis); parasympathetic via CN III (short ciliary nerve)
Tap
Dilator Pupillae
Radially arranged myoepithelial cells in stroma (ectoderm-derived); thin layer
Tap
Dilates pupil (mydriasis); sympathetic innervation (cervical ganglion)
Tap
Double Pigmented Epithelium
Two layers of heavily melanin-pigmented cells on posterior iris surface
Tap
Absorbs stray light; inner layer modified into dilator pupillae; outer layer continues with non-pigmented ciliary epithelium
Tap
👁️ Pupillary Autonomics — High Yield
Parasympathetic (CN III → ciliary ganglion → short ciliary n.): sphincter pupillae → miosis; ciliary muscle → accommodation.
Sympathetic (T1 → superior cervical ganglion → long ciliary n.): dilator pupillae → mydriasis; Müller's muscle → lid elevation.
Loss of sympathetics = Horner's syndrome: ptosis + miosis + anhidrosis.
Fig 9 (Manual) Iris: chromophores in stroma, dilator pupillae, sphincter pupillae, double pigmented epithelium (H&E, Iowa Collection) Medical Histology Manual, Week 42

The Lens

A biconvex, avascular, transparent structure suspended in the posterior chamber by zonular fibers. It provides ~17D of variable refractive power. It is an entirely epithelial structure — essentially a highly modified epithelial sheet.

Lens Structure

ComponentHistologyFunction / Clinical
Capsule
Thick basement membrane (Type IV collagen, laminin, fibronectin); thickest BM in body
Tap
Anchors zonule fibers; maintains lens shape. Torn during cataract surgery (phacoemulsification)
Tap
Lens Epithelium
Simple cuboidal monolayer on anterior surface only; cells proliferate at equator (germinative zone)
Tap
Lifelong addition of new lens fibers at equator → lens grows throughout life; anterior subcapsular cataracts arise from epithelial changes
Tap
Lens Fibers
Elongated, anucleate cells packed with crystallin proteins; connected by gap junctions and desmosomes
Tap
Crystallins provide transparency & appropriate refractive index. Denaturation/aggregation → cataract
Tap
Equator / Nuclear Zone
Transitional region where epithelial cells elongate and lose nuclei to form lens fibers; nuclei form "lens bow" arc
Tap
Zonule fibers attach here; equatorial epithelium has proliferative potential → posterior capsule opacification after surgery
Tap
🔭 Accommodation & Presbyopia
Near focus: ciliary muscle contracts → zonules relax → lens springs into rounder shape (↑ curvature → ↑ power).
Distance: ciliary muscle relaxes → zonules taut → lens flattened.
Presbyopia: lens nucleus hardens with age → loses elasticity → near focus fails even though ciliary muscle works. Fixed with reading glasses (convex lens adds power).
Fig 10 (Manual) Equatorial pole of lens — capsule, epithelium, nuclear zone, zonule fibers, iris (H&E) Medical Histology Manual, Week 42

The Retina — Ten Layers

The retina is an outgrowth of the diencephalon — it is brain tissue. It lines the inner 4/5 of the globe from the optic disc to the ora serrata. Its ten layers run from innermost (facing vitreous) to outermost (facing choroid).

🧠 Orientation
Light travels inward (vitreous → nerve fiber layer → through all layers) and hits photoreceptors last. The retina is inverted — photoreceptors face away from the light. The pigment epithelium is outermost, absorbing excess photons and recycling visual pigment.

The Ten Layers (Inside → Outside)

#Layer NameContents / HistologyFunction
10Internal Limiting Membrane
Basal lamina of Müller cells + astrocyte endfeet; innermost boundary
Tap
Retina–vitreous interface; surgical peeling target in macular pucker repair
Tap
9Nerve Fiber Layer (ONFL)
Unmyelinated axons of retinal ganglion cells coursing toward optic disc
Tap
Forms optic nerve; thinning = glaucoma damage (measured by OCT)
Tap
8Ganglion Cell Layer (RGCL)
Cell bodies of retinal ganglion cells; large nuclei; sparse (1/ganglion cell)
Tap
Output neurons of retina → axons form optic nerve → lateral geniculate nucleus
Tap
7Inner Plexiform Layer (IPL)
Synaptic connections between bipolar cells, amacrine cells, and ganglion cell dendrites
Tap
2nd-order synaptic processing; modulation by amacrine cells
Tap
6Inner Nuclear Layer (INL)
Cell bodies of bipolar cells, amacrine cells, horizontal cells, and Müller cell nuclei
Tap
Interneurons; Müller cells are radial glia spanning entire retinal thickness
Tap
5Outer Plexiform Layer (OPL)
Synaptic contacts between photoreceptor axon terminals (spherules/pedicles) and bipolar/horizontal cell dendrites
Tap
1st synapse in visual pathway; horizontal cells mediate lateral inhibition
Tap
4Outer Nuclear Layer (ONL)
Cell bodies (nuclei) of rods and cones; cones at fovea, rods peripherally
Tap
Photoreceptor nuclei; densest at macula
Tap
3Outer Limiting Membrane
Zonulae adherentes between Müller cell apical processes and photoreceptor inner segments; not a true membrane
Tap
Forms apical boundary of retinal cell bodies; part of blood–retina barrier
Tap
2Photoreceptor Layer (PRL)
Inner and outer segments of rods and cones; outer segments contain membranous discs with visual pigment
Tap
Phototransduction — photons close cGMP-gated channels → hyperpolarize cell
Tap
1Retinal Pigment Epithelium (RPE)
Single layer of cuboidal cells packed with melanin; sit on Bruch's membrane; tight junctions (outer blood–retina barrier)
Tap
Phagocytose shed outer segment discs; regenerate 11-cis retinal; absorb stray light; maintain outer blood–retina barrier
Tap

Support Cells of the Retina

🧩 Müller Cells Radial Macroglia Span entire retinal thickness; provide structural support; recycle glutamate and K⁺; maintain blood–retina barrier
⭐ Astrocytes Nerve fiber layer glia Perivascular; support ganglion cell axons; regulate blood flow
🔷 Microglia Resident macrophages Immune surveillance; activated in retinal disease (AMD, diabetic retinopathy)
🎨 RPE Cells Pigment epithelium Non-neuronal support; drusen accumulation → AMD; disruption → vision loss
Fig 11 (Manual) Ten layers of the retina (labeled) with underlying choroid and optic disc inset below (H&E) Medical Histology Manual, Week 42

Photoreceptors — Rods & Cones

Two types of photoreceptors convert light into electrical signals. They share the same basic structure — outer segment (OS), inner segment (IS), cell body, and synaptic terminal — but differ in morphology, pigment, and visual function.

FeatureRodsCones
Number
~120 million
Tap
~7 million
Tap
Peak density
~20° from fovea (parafoveal ring); absent from foveal center
Tap
Fovea centralis (fovea has ONLY cones); density drops sharply peripherally
Tap
Outer segment shape
Long, cylindrical; membranous discs completely pinched off from plasma membrane
Tap
Shorter, tapered (cone-shaped); discs continuous with plasma membrane (open infoldings)
Tap
Visual pigment
Rhodopsin = scotopsin + 11-cis retinal; one type
Tap
Iodopsin (photopsins); 3 types: S-cone (blue, ~420nm), M-cone (green, ~530nm), L-cone (red, ~560nm)
Tap
Light sensitivity
Extremely sensitive; function in dim light (scotopic vision)
Tap
Less sensitive; require bright light (photopic vision)
Tap
Color
B&W / grayscale only
Tap
Color discrimination (trichromatic vision)
Tap
Synaptic terminal
Rod spherule — spherical ending with 1 ribbon synapse
Tap
Cone pedicle — flat base with multiple (up to 20+) ribbon synapses
Tap
Convergence
High convergence (many rods → 1 bipolar); enhances sensitivity, reduces acuity
Tap
Low convergence at fovea (1:1 cone:bipolar:ganglion); maximizes acuity
Tap

Phototransduction (simplified)

Photon hits 11-cis retinal → Isomerizes to all-trans retinal → Activates opsin → transducin (G-protein) → Phosphodiesterase activated → cGMP↓ → cGMP-gated Na⁺ channels close → Hyperpolarization → ↓ Glutamate release → bipolar cell activation
🌑 Fovea & Macula
The fovea centralis is a pit-like depression (~1.5 mm diameter) at the center of the macula (~5 mm diameter). At the foveal center (foveola), all inner retinal layers are displaced laterally — only cone photoreceptors remain. This, combined with 1:1 wiring, gives maximum visual acuity. The fovea is the point where gaze is directed during fixation. It is avascular (nourished by choroid).

The Optic Nerve & Disc

The optic nerve is not a true peripheral nerve — it is a white matter tract of the brain, surrounded by the three meningeal layers (pia, arachnoid, dura) and a continuation of the subarachnoid space. Its axons are those of retinal ganglion cells and are unmyelinated within the retina, acquiring myelin after passing through the lamina cribrosa.

Key Structures

StructureHistologyClinical Significance
Optic Disc
Region where axons converge and exit the globe; no photoreceptors (physiological blind spot)
Tap
Papilloedema = disc swelling from raised ICP (CSF pressure transmitted via subarachnoid space around optic nerve)
Tap
Optic Cup
Central depression in optic disc; normally cup:disc ratio <0.5
Tap
Enlarged cup (↑ C:D ratio) in glaucoma = loss of neuroretinal rim from ganglion cell axon death
Tap
Lamina Cribrosa
Fenestrated sieve-like plate of collagen in sclera; axons pass through pores
Tap
Pressure difference across lamina (IOP vs tissue pressure) stresses axons; glaucoma damage occurs here
Tap
Meningeal Sheaths
Pia (adherent to nerve) → subarachnoid space → arachnoid → dura; continuous with intracranial meninges
Tap
Raised ICP → increased subarachnoid pressure → axonal transport block → disc swelling (papilloedema). MS plaques can form in optic nerve (optic neuritis)
Tap
Central Retinal Artery & Vein
Enter/exit nerve ~1 cm behind globe; course through center of nerve to emerge at disc
Tap
Central retinal artery occlusion → sudden painless monocular blindness; cherry-red spot at fovea. Visible on fundoscopy at disc
Tap
👁️ Clinical — Optic Neuritis
The optic nerve is surrounded by myelin (oligodendrocytes). Demyelination in multiple sclerosis → optic neuritis: painful monocular vision loss, reduced color vision (especially red desaturation), relative afferent pupillary defect (RAPD/Marcus Gunn pupil). Vision usually recovers. First presentation of MS in ~25% of cases.
Fig 12 (Manual) Optic nerve and disc — axons of retinal ganglion cells, lamina cribrosa, pia and dura meninges (H&E, Iowa Collection) Medical Histology Manual, Week 42
